full_response:
Let g_{ab} be the (pseudo-)Riemannian metric. 
Because g_{ab}=g_{ba} and g^{ac}g_{cb}=\delta^{a}_{\;b}, the metric can be used to raise or lower indices:

T^{ab}=g^{ac}g^{bd}\,T_{cd},     T_{ab}=g_{ac}g_{bd}\,T^{cd}.        (1)

Throughout, repeated indices are summed and all free indices keep their order.

1.  Antisymmetric case  

Assume T_{ab} is purely antisymmetric:

T_{ab}=T_{[ab]}\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad T_{ab}=-T_{ba}.        (2)

Raise both indices with (1):

T^{ab}=g^{ac}g^{bd}T_{cd}.                                            (3)

Now interchange a↔b in (3):

T^{ba}=g^{bc}g^{ad}T_{cd}
      =g^{bc}g^{ad}(-T_{dc})        (use (2))
      =-g^{bc}g^{ad}T_{dc}.                                            (4)

Relabel the dummy indices c↔d in the last term; because g^{bc}=g^{cb},

T^{ba}=-g^{bd}g^{ac}T_{cd}=-T^{ab}.                                   (5)

Hence T^{ab}=-T^{ba}, i.e. T^{ab}=T^{[ab]}.  

Conversely, if T^{ab}=T^{[ab]}, then lowering with (1) (and repeating the same steps) gives T_{ab}=T_{[ab]}. Therefore

T_{ab}=T_{[ab]}\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;T^{ab}=T^{[ab]}.            (6)

Because the metric is symmetric, raising or lowering a pair of indices preserves antisymmetry.

2.  Symmetric case  

Let S_{ab}=S_{(ab)} (so S_{ab}=S_{ba}).  Repeating the calculation above but without the minus sign:

S^{ba}=g^{bc}g^{ad}S_{cd}=g^{bc}g^{ad}S_{dc}=g^{ad}g^{bc}S_{cd}=S^{ab}.    (7)

Hence S^{ab}=S^{(ab)}.  The converse again holds, so symmetry is also preserved.

Therefore raising and lowering indices with the (symmetric) metric preserves both antisymmetry and symmetry of a pair of indices.

\boxed{\;T_{ab}=T_{[ab]}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;T^{ab}=T^{[ab]} \quad\text{and likewise}\quad T_{ab}=T_{(ab)}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;T^{ab}=T^{(ab)}\;}

Confidence: 92%
